one_line_takeaway: FinSafetyBench shows that even finance-tuned and frontier LLMs (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-V3.2) remain substantially jailbreakable on real-world-grounded financial-crime and ethics-violation prompts, with robustness tracking safety alignment and domain tuning rather than model scale, and with existing prompt-level defenses (Self-Reminder, ICD, and the authors' own Fin-Guard) failing to reliably stop iterative (PAIR/ReNeLLM) or obfuscation-based (FlipAttack) attacks.
key_finding: (1) Jailbreak vulnerability is severe and largely attack- and model-dependent rather than scale-dependent: Mistral-Small-24B is the most vulnerable model overall (average ASR >90% in both languages on financial crimes: 93.55% En, 91.46% Zh), while LLaMA-3-8B is the most robust (lowest ASRs: 32.29% En / 52.65% Zh on financial crimes) despite being the smallest model, and the finance-tuned XuanYuan-13B shows comparatively higher robustness than several larger general models (57.47% En / 69.58% Zh on financial crimes), which the authors interpret as evidence that safety alignment and domain grounding, not parameter count, drive robustness -- reinforced by a stated Qwen2.5-32B vs 72B comparison showing no safety gain from scaling (detail in an appendix not captured in this extraction). (2) Models are consistently more vulnerable on Chinese than English data for financial-crime prompts in aggregate (Figure 3 category averages skew higher in Chinese for most subcategories), though the per-model breakdown in Table 2 shows this direction is not universal (e.g., Mistral and Qwen2.5 are actually somewhat more vulnerable in English). (3) Iterative attacks (PAIR, ReNeLLM) are the most consistently effective; FlipAttack is highly unstable, ranging from 93.32% ASR against Mistral down to 6.52% against XuanYuan on financial crimes (En), attributed to XuanYuan's tendency to over-attend to the literal character-flip instruction rather than the underlying malicious intent. (4) Financial Crimes are consistently more jailbreakable than Ethical Violations across models and languages (e.g., category averages in Figure 3 cluster around 70-80% for crimes subcategories such as False Invoicing and Misappropriation of Funds vs. lower for ethics subcategories), attributed to crime procedures being more extensively and explicitly documented in training-adjacent corpora (judicial/regulatory text) than abstract ethical norms. (5) Frontier closed models remain vulnerable: under PAIR on a 25-instance/category subsample, DeepSeek-V3.2 (685B) shows 89.45% ASR on financial crimes and 70.67% on ethics; GPT-5.1 shows a much lower 35.27% on financial crimes but a comparably high 66.67% on ethics. (6) Prompt-level defenses are inconsistent and often insufficient: on Qwen2.5-32B (Chinese, financial crimes) Self-Reminder cut ReNeLLM ASR from 68.2% to 29.3%, and the authors' Fin-Guard cut PAIR ASR from 81.7% to 47.7%; but on GLM-4-9B the same Fin-Guard only reduced PAIR ASR from 90.1% to 72.2%, and no defense meaningfully mitigated FlipAttack on either model. (7) The strict LLM-judge protocol (Judge-Main) is corroborated by three alternative judging schemes (Judge-Dict, Judge-Rubric, Judge-Harm) all yielding similarly high ASRs on the Qwen2.5+FlipAttack condition (Table 4: Judge-Main 93.06/92.33/71.34/68.58 vs. e.g. Judge-Rubric 95.96/93.96/80.84/66.52 across En-Crimes/Zh-Crimes/En-Ethics/Zh-Ethics), though Judge-Dict (keyword matching) correlates poorly with the LLM-based judges, consistent with known limitations of keyword-based refusal detection.
core_contribution: The paper introduces FINSAFETYBENCH, a bilingual (English-Chinese) red-teaming benchmark of 1,881 instances for evaluating whether LLMs refuse requests that facilitate financial crimes or financial ethics violations. Unlike prior financial safety benchmarks that test regulatory knowledge or are built from LLM-synthesized data, FinSafetyBench is grounded in 1,201 proprietary items derived from real-world judicial/legal case materials and CFA mock exam questions (extracted and summarized with Qwen3-32B, then rephrased into harmful instructions with DeepSeek-V3, with harmfulness verified by checking that Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct reliably refuses them), plus 680 items filtered/re-verified from three external red-teaming datasets (TRIDENT, HarmfulQA, HExPHI). The taxonomy has two top-level categories -- 11 subtypes of financial crimes (e.g., Market Manipulation, Bribery, Insider Trading, Misappropriation of Funds, Fraud, Tax Evasion, Forgery, Money Laundering, Misrepresentation, False Invoicing, Cybercrime) and 3 subtypes of ethical violations (Harming Customer Interests, Harming Employer Interests, Lack of Independence and Objectivity) -- for 14 subcategories total. Using the benchmark, the authors run three black-box jailbreak attacks (PAIR, ReNeLLM, FlipAttack) against six open models (LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, InternLM3-8B-Instruct, GLM-4-9B-0414, Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, and the finance-tuned XuanYuan-13B-Chat), extend to two closed frontier models (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-V3.2) on a 25-instance/category subsample under PAIR, evaluate three prompt-level defenses (Self-Reminder, In-Context Defense, and a novel domain-specific defense the authors call Fin-Guard), and run a judge-robustness ablation comparing their LLM judge (Qwen3-32B, strict score=10 threshold) against three alternative judging schemes (keyword-based Judge-Dict, five-point Judge-Rubric, binary Judge-Harm).
threat_model: Black-box, prompt-only adversary: an attacker submits natural-language jailbreak prompts (no gradient access, no white-box optimization) to a target LLM (open-weight or API-served) aiming to elicit a fully actionable response (judged score=10 on a 1-10 scale) to a financially harmful request spanning either serious financial crime (e.g., insider trading, money laundering, market manipulation) or professional ethics violations (e.g., undisclosed conflicts of interest, misrepresenting risk to clients). Three concrete attack families are used as the operationalized threat: PAIR (iterative attacker-model-in-the-loop prompt refinement, n_streams=5, n_iterations=3, attack model Qwen3-32B), ReNeLLM (prompt rewriting + scenario nesting, iter_max=3, attack model Qwen3-32B), and FlipAttack (reversible character-level obfuscation, flip_mode=FCS, cot=lang_gpt). Defenses considered are all prompt-level/inference-time (system-prompt reminders or few-shot in-context demonstrations), not weight-level fine-tuning or output filtering.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) only two languages (English and Chinese) are covered, omitting other languages; (2) model-assisted steps (LLM rephrasing, LLM-based translation) may introduce subtle generation biases despite manual human validation; (3) the study evaluates only a selected set of LLMs (8B-32B open models plus two frontier closed models under a reduced sampling protocol) and three jailbreak strategies, potentially missing emerging attack or defense techniques. Additional caveats visible from the text itself but not explicitly flagged by the authors: the closed-frontier-model results (Table 3) use only a 25-instances-per-category subsample and only the PAIR attack, so they are not directly comparable in statistical power to the full six-model/three-attack open-model results; the defense analysis (Figure 4) is restricted to only two of the six models (Qwen2.5, GLM-4) and only Chinese data, limiting generalizability of defense-effectiveness claims; the harmfulness-verification step used the same model family (Qwen) both to generate seed rephrasings/verification and later as an evaluation judge in places, which could introduce correlated bias between data construction and evaluation, though a separate model (DeepSeek-V3) was used for the rephrasing step itself; the strict ASR criterion (score=10 only) is a deliberate design choice acknowledged by the authors, with partial-jailbreak analysis deferred to an appendix not captured in this extraction.

datasets_used: FINSAFETYBENCH (this paper's own benchmark): 1,881 bilingual (En/Zh) instances, 1,201 proprietary (from real-world financial judicial/legal case materials and public CFA mock exam items) + 680 from external sources
TRIDENT (Hui et al. 2025) -- external red-teaming dataset, financial subset filtered and integrated
HarmfulQA (Bhardwaj and Poria 2023) -- external red-teaming dataset, financial subset filtered and integrated
HExPHI (Qi et al. 2024) -- external red-teaming dataset, financial subset filtered and integrated
quantitative_results: Table 2 (average ASR %, PAIR/ReNeLLM/FlipAttack averaged, En/Zh, Financial Crimes / Ethical Violations): LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct 32.29/52.65 (crimes En/Zh), 40.56/42.57 (ethics En/Zh); InternLM3-8B-Instruct 72.18/64.96, 49.39/46.92; GLM-4-9B-0414 72.34/87.02, 56.91/61.59; Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501 93.55/91.46, 78.95/70.87; Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct 88.18/80.73, 72.01/65.53; XuanYuan-13B-Chat 57.47/69.58, 45.06/48.52

Table 3 (closed frontier models, PAIR only, 25 instances/category subsample, average ASR %): GPT-5.1 35.27 (Financial Crimes) / 66.67 (Ethical Violations); DeepSeek-V3.2 (685B) 89.45 (Financial Crimes) / 70.67 (Ethical Violations)

Table 4 (judge-robustness ablation, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct + FlipAttack, ASR % En-Crimes/Zh-Crimes/En-Ethics/Zh-Ethics): Judge-Main 93.06/92.33/71.34/68.58; Judge-Dict 80.44/97.65/86.07/96.98; Judge-Rubric 95.96/93.96/80.84/66.52; Judge-Harm 87.29/85.76/78.21/74.42
FlipAttack instability: 93.32% ASR against Mistral vs 6.52% against XuanYuan (Financial Crimes, English)
Human-judge agreement with the Qwen3-32B automated judge: 93.6% on a stratified sample
Defense effect on Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct (Chinese, Financial Crimes, Figure 4): Self-Reminder reduces ReNeLLM ASR from 68.2% (vanilla) to 29.3%; Fin-Guard reduces PAIR ASR from 81.7% to 47.7%

Defense effect on GLM-4-9B-0414 (Chinese, Financial Crimes, Figure 4): Fin-Guard reduces PAIR ASR from 90.1% (vanilla) to only 72.2%; no defense strategy meaningfully reduced FlipAttack ASR on either model
baselines_compared: Jailbreak attacks: PAIR (Chao et al. 2025), ReNeLLM, FlipAttack (Liu et al. 2024c)
Defenses: Self-Reminder (Xie et al. 2023), In-Context Defense/ICD (Wei et al. 2023), Fin-Guard (the paper's own domain-specific defense)

Judging methods: Judge-Main / PAIR-style LLM judge (Chao et al. 2025), Judge-Dict keyword matching (Zou et al. 2023), Judge-Rubric 5-point scale (Qi et al. 2024), Judge-Harm binary (Mazeika et al. 2024)

Target models: LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, InternLM3-8B-Instruct, GLM-4-9B-0414, Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, XuanYuan-13B-Chat (finance-domain), plus closed frontier GPT-5.1 and DeepSeek-V3.2 (685B)
Prior financial benchmarks compared for positioning (not re-run experimentally, comparison is descriptive via Table 1): FinEval, SuperCLUE-Fin, CFinBench, ASSEBench, FinBen, TRIDENT, OpenFinData
